briefing.opening: Good morning, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for March 30, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 The Severity of the Climate Crisis and Its Immense Economic Damage
sections.content: According to a report released today, greenhouse gas emissions over the past 30 years have caused an estimated $51 trillion in economic damage [2]. This clearly demonstrates that climate change is no longer merely an environmental issue but has a profound impact on the daily lives and economies of people worldwide [79]. Island nations, such as Comoros, are urging emergency measures, stating that their lives are deteriorating and cultural heritage is threatened by climate change, while Haiti is also experiencing disproportionate suffering [78, 80, 89]. Furthermore, climate change is linked to increased cancer incidence and the spread of superbugs due to antibiotic resistance, posing a severe threat to human health [77, 92]. A minister emphasized that climate change is no longer a threat but an unavoidable reality, indicating that now is the time for immediate action [94].

sections.title: ⚠️ Accelerating Energy Transition and Facing Challenges
sections.content: As the global energy crisis intensifies due to energy shocks from the Middle East, key domestic and international figures are repeatedly urging a rapid transition to renewable energy [35, 36, 38, 40, 41, 43, 45, 49, 50, 51, 53, 56, 57, 58, 59, 61]. President Lee, in particular, described the severity of the energy issue as keeping him awake at night, emphasizing that Jeju Island must be made a 'first mover' in renewable energy transition [53, 61]. However, numerous policy challenges remain, including arguments that the coexistence of renewable energy and nuclear power is virtually impossible during the energy transition [31]. Furthermore, according to the Sustainable Development Implementation Report, Korean society has shown insufficient improvement and even regression in the renewable energy sector, alongside issues of wealth disparity and gender inequality [32, 70]. Amidst this situation, Korea's renewable energy policy is undergoing a major transformation, including the full-scale reorganization of the Electric Power Industry Base Fund in preparation for the abolition of RPS (Renewable Portfolio Standard), making future policy direction and implementation even more critical [66].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic Efforts for Carbon Neutrality and Renewable Energy Expansion
sections.content: Even amidst this crisis, diverse efforts to achieve carbon neutrality are continuing domestically. POSCO is leading climate change response by commencing mass production of carbon-reducing steel plates [3], and Chonnam National University's research team is actively seeking technological solutions, such as presenting the possibility of maintaining crop productivity amidst the climate crisis [5]. Local governments and businesses are also actively participating: Dobong-gu is sharing its environmental education city operation cases [7], Seongdong-gu is supporting small business owners in purchasing high-efficiency equipment [12], and Sampyo Group is strengthening carbon neutrality education [10, 14]. Notably, Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEPCO) has lifted power regulations in the Jeju region and resumed reviewing new renewable energy generation permits, expecting Jeju to become a crucial hub for expanding solar and wind power generation and carbon-neutral energy transition [29, 34, 42, 60, 62, 64]. Furthermore, everyday carbon neutrality practices, such as the Korea Forest Welfare Institute's planting of 10,000 saplings and Jeonbuk Bank's tree-planting volunteer activities, are steadily expanding [15, 18, 23, 24, 25].

sections.title: 🌍 Global Climate Adaptation and Scientific Response
sections.content: The international community continues its efforts to enhance scientific understanding of climate change and explore practical adaptation measures. Unique experiments tracking plant evolutionary responses to climate change have been conducted in 30 regions worldwide [74], and ski resorts in New Hampshire are adapting to climate change with advanced snowmaking technology [71]. Furthermore, research highlighting beavers as providing nature-based solutions to combat climate change by building wetland dams is also gaining attention [82, 85, 88]. In addition, the importance of gender equality and land rights in climate change response is being emphasized [75, 76, 93], and attempts are being made to raise public awareness through climate entertainment [72]. Hallym University is fostering global talent for climate change response, and efforts are being made to strengthen the capabilities of future generations, including the announcement of climate change and press freedom fellowship opportunities targeting the Global South [1, 91].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has once again highlighted the severity of the climate crisis and its immense economic damage, while also showcasing strong domestic and international commitment and various practical examples for the transition to renewable energy. Global cooperation and innovative efforts to address climate change must continue in the future. This concludes today's climate briefing.
